The Economist's story about China's trade gap -- based on work by Adam Wolfe -- is in my view misleading.   Import prices increased by more than export prices; the real trade surplus is still expanding (see the net export contribution in q1/q2)

1/

China isn't ending its capital controls, nor should the world be asking it too --

So long as the yuan is fixed stronger (appreciating) exporters want to hold yuan, and "private" money flows into China, leading to a surge in settlement.

This is actually predictable!

1/2

"China’s trade gap is narrowing. And other surprises
The world’s second-biggest economy is stumbling into fiscal austerity" https://t.co/lUWtAsNbbx https://t.co/cn5gXJeiqh
Fascinating chart by @Brad_Setser. Conventional wisdom has been that if China ended capital controls and FX intervention, yuan would depreciate due to capital flight. Maybe that's no longer true.

I should probably do a blog on this -- but this week's WSJ story was a better representation what is going on in China than this Economist piece.   Export growth continues at a ~ 10% y/y pace; far father than the global economy

6/6

WSJ: "Washington argues that PIX has become so ubiquitous in Brazil it unfairly disadvantages U.S. payment companies such as Visa and Mastercard."

There are good reasons for tariffs and bad reasons for tariffs, but this has to be among the most foolish.
https://t.co/to2rXeUM8U

Three months ago PIEE's Maurice Obstfeld wrote yet another piece arguing that the cause of the US trade deficit is (what else?) not enough fiscal austerity. He argues that if the US were to reduce its fiscal deficit, it would cause a rise in US saving, and so less American borrowing from foreigners and a corresponding drop in the US trade deficits.

Like many American economists Obstfeld does not seem to believe that foreigners have agency. He thinks that foreigners invest in the US mainly to accommodate the needs of Americans, and not to accommodate their own needs – in this case to acquire assets to balance their persistent trade surpluses.

So if the US were to reduce its fiscal deficit, as Obstfeld (and so many others) insist, would that really cause foreigners to stop reconciling their trade surpluses with the purchase of US assets? 

Almost certainly not, unless fiscal austerity succeeds in driving a sharp-enough economic contraction and raising US unemployment. Even then, it might just shift foreign purchases out of American stocks and into US bonds, leaving the US worse off economically and the trade deficit as big as ever. Countries that run trade surpluses, after all, must acquire foreign assets to balance them, and it is unlikely that a smaller US fiscal deficit will cause them to change their minds about the safety of US assets.

For those who are interested, Michael McNair responded at the time with a piece explaining the various confusions in the Obstfeld piece. It was published nearly three months ago, and is a little technical, but I am reposting it because this is one of those very important topics in which an ideological commitment to austerity should not be allowed to overrule our understanding of global trade and capital flows.
https://t.co/5j8gaIg5LN

Important point on how a country's trade surplus and its financial surplus can be directed to different countries, forcing the overall imbalances to overcome the original bilateral imbalances.

Put another way, if China runs trade surpluses with Europe, but balances these surpluses by acquiring US assets, Europe will have balanced trade overall, while the US will run the corresponding overall deficit.

Caixin: "Chinese households are spending the smallest share of their incomes since the depths of the pandemic. While per capita disposable income rose 4.2% year-on-year in real terms in the first half of the year, consumer spending lagged far behind with just a 2.7% increase."

Caixin notes that while households consumed 68-69% of their income before the pandemic, they are now consuming less than 65%, even as banks are under pressure to expand consumer lending. This probably reflects rising worries about job prospects.

For all the policy talk about boosting the role of consumption in driving growth, in other words, the economy is moving in the opposite direction. Not only is disposable household income rising more slowly in real terms than than GDP (4.7% in the first six months of 2026), but households are also saving a higher share of their income.

As the household share of GDP declines, there are only two ways to balance this. One way is for the investment share to rise, which hasn't been happening recently and, given how few productive investment opportunities remain in China, is unlikely to happen without an even greater surge in debt.

The other way, of course, is for the share of net exports in driving GDP to rise.
https://t.co/gZAxC5CwjM

This is concerning. For the first time, a Chinese model Kimi K3 has taken #1 on the Frontend Code Arena and is scoring at or near the frontier on other benchmarks.  

Meanwhile America is tying itself in knots: politicians and bureaucrats are banning new data centers, piling on state regulations, and pushing for new federal agencies to pre-approve frontier models. 

This is how you lose the AI race. The rest of the world won’t play by our rules if we bog ourselves down. Permissionless innovation is how America won the internet and became the technological envy of the world. We can do it again with AI -- while addressing risks in a targeted way -- or we’ll watch our lead evaporate.

KFC hired 23 of 24 applicants in Jack Ma's city. He was the 1 rejection. He later ran a $25 billion IPO.

The clip is Ma at Davos, 2015, reading out his rejection resume with a grin.

Harvard: applied 10 times, rejected 10 times.

The police: 5 classmates applied, 4 got in. His rejection came with a review: "you're no good."

KFC arrives in his city: 24 apply, 23 hired. He's the leftover, again.

Even the future disagreed with him. In 1996 he pitched internet businesses door to door in Hangzhou and got called a con man.

In 1999 he gathered 17 friends in his apartment and founded Alibaba on $60,000 of pooled savings.

In 2014 it listed in New York at $25 billion, the largest IPO in history at the time. His English-teacher salary had been $12 a month.

His Davos advice from the pile of rejections: get used to it, the no's are training.

Then the sequel proved him right again. A 2020 speech criticizing regulators cost him the $317 billion Ant listing, and he vanished from public view for months.

Rejected by KFC, erased by Beijing. Still worth $25 billion.

Every door closed on him once. He owns the building now.
